title: Six Open Questions in Machine-Learned Interatomic Potential Foundation Models
authors: Isabel Creed, Tim Rein, Ingvars Vitenburgs, Wojciech G. Stark, Viktor Ellingsson, Ahmed Y. Ismail, Guangyu Liu, Yuchen Lou, Bradley A. A. Martin, Cyprien Bone, Matthew A. H. Walker, Mueen Taj, Shirui Wang, Kelvin Wong, Ruiqi Wu, Prakriti Kayastha, Bingqing Cheng, Aditi Krishnapriyan, Michele Ceriotti, Marcel F. Langer, Jarvist Moore Frost, Alex M. Ganose, Venkat Kapil, Keith T. Butler
abstract: Machine-learned interatomic potentials (MLIPs) have had a profound impact on molecular modelling in recent years, promising to resolve the long-standing tension between the scale and accuracy of simulations. There has been a proliferation of new models and designs, and recently the paradigm of ``foundational'' MLIPs has become prevalent. Broadly speaking, foundation models are trained on large diverse datasets and promise to work well for new systems with minimal updates required. However, in such a new and fast moving field, there are many unanswered questions. In this article, we set out to articulate and explore what we see as the most important among these questions. We start by developing a working definition for foundational MLIPs and use this definition to frame the subsequent open questions. Despite the rapid progress in the field of MLIP models, we believe that these are fundamental questions which will continue to define cutting edge research in MLIPs in the years to come.
summary: This paper surveys the rapidly advancing field of Machine-Learned Interatomic Potentials (MLIPs), which promise to bridge the gap between computational scale and accuracy in materials science simulations. It synthesizes current knowledge by defining a set of six fundamental, unanswered questions that researchers must tackle regarding model expressivity, data requirements, and physical generalization.
main_problem: The field of Machine-Learned Interatomic Potentials (MLIPs) needs to define its fundamental boundaries by addressing several open questions regarding model design, data requirements, and physical scope.
main_result: The paper articulates six key open questions—covering expressivity, scalability, memorization, multimodality, and compositionality—that must guide future research in foundation MLIP models.
method: A conceptual framework is developed by adapting general AI foundation model taxonomies to the specific physical constraints and mathematical structures of interatomic potentials.
model_or_system: The focus is on Machine-Learned Interatomic Potentials (MLIPs) used to approximate the Born-Oppenheimer potential energy surface for molecular and materials systems. These models aim to replace traditional, physically parameterized force fields.
key_observables: Potential energy, atomic forces, stresses, thermodynamic properties, and prediction accuracy across various physical regimes (e.g., defect formation energies).
important_parameters: Model expressivity, dataset size/diversity, computational cost, and the incorporation of physical symmetries like $E(3)$-equivariance.
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that progress in MLIPs requires addressing fundamental theoretical limitations rather than just incremental architectural improvements or data scaling alone.
figures_summary: Figure 1 maps general AI foundation model criteria (expressivity, etc.) to the six open questions for MLIPs. Other figures illustrate performance gains versus training set size using specific architectures like NequIP.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the context of MLIPs, defines a working concept of 'foundation' models, and then systematically explores and articulates the six most critical unanswered questions defining the cutting edge of research in this domain.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter, the rigorous framework for analyzing model limitations (expressivity vs. data scaling) mirrors challenges in developing complex quantum simulation methods that rely on scalable neural representations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. The connections between definitions and open questions. The text of the article has been analysed using a cosine similarity of vectorized embeddings. On the left, we show how the definition criteria link to the questions, on the right, the relations between the questions (colour-coded as on the left) are displayed. The line widths reflect the similarity of the embeddings of the content.

Figure 2. Log-log plot of the predictive error on the water data set from [59] using NequIP with rotation order L ∈{0, 1, 2, 3} as a function of training set size, measured via the force MAE. Figure from Batzner et al. [32]

Figure 3. Overview of conceptual approaches to accurately capture long range interactions in GNNs while mitigating oversmoothing and oversquashing. Strategies include: Graph Rewiring (top left) for structural optimization; Dual Architectures (top right) for global-local separation; Hierarchical Approaches (bottom right) for multi-scale feature extraction; and incorporation of Physics-Informed Priors that encode the long-range governing physics (bottom left).

Figure 4. Different resolutions of lipid membranes. All-atom (AA) resolution explicitly considers all atoms. Coarse-grain (CG) resolution considers small atom groups and their associated hydrogens. Supra-CG resolution represents solvents implicitly and proteins and lipids as qualitative few-bead models. Implicit resolution further integrates out lipid molecules. Reprinted from [197].

Figure 5. Combined performance score against the model size (number of model parameters) obtained with different foundation MLIPs, based on Matbench Discovery [38] benchmark website (https://matbench-discovery.materialsproject.org, November 2025).
